source_title: #385 - AMA #82: Applying the tools of longevity in the real world: disease prevention, DEXA scans, artificial sweeteners, injury recovery, stability training, habit formation, protein intake and mTOR activation, and more

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: I don't work with 20-year-olds typically and therefore I don't think I have the breadth of experience to really speak intelligently at that age range the way I do for people in their 40s and above. The median age of my patient is in the mid-50s with an interquartile range of, call it, 40 to 70. What I would say is that you can get away with so much in your 20s. This is the period of time in which you can overtrain, you can expand the envelope of your capacity. I know that the reason to this day, despite the fact that I don't train that hard, I still have a much higher VO2 max than would be predicted I think by my training volume. I owe that all to what I was doing when I was young.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: When people get into their 40s, most people start to have that first brush with mortality. Part of that is external. You might be watching your parents' age or things of that nature. But I think also part of it is internal. By the late 40s that becomes true. Patients will say, look, man, there was a day when I could throw down three drinks a night and feel nothing. And now I just have a headache the next day, or I don't sleep well, and I don't perform well the next day. By the time you're in your 40s, you really need to be thinking about what am I doing from a prevention standpoint? Am I metabolically healthy? Is hypertension starting to creep up? This is really a time where you don't want to start missing workouts. You're better off doing something very frequently and not getting out of the habit than you are taking long periods of time off and then coming back and trying to be heroic.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: Once you get into your 60s and beyond, the name of the game is maintenance if you've done a good job until that point. But the good news is if you haven't, there's still an enormous opportunity for growth. If you've come into the seventh and eighth decade of your life not particularly healthy, that should not be viewed as a scenario by which you decide, well, the die has been cast and away I go. We've seen patients that come into our practice in their 60s with a VO2 max in the high teens, low 20s. That doesn't prevent us from training them and getting them to a much higher level of fitness within two or three years.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: The big four killers in chronic disease land are: atherosclerotic, cardiovascular, and cerebrovascular disease being one. Cancer. All of the neurodegenerative diseases and dementing diseases is the third. And then the foundation upon which these all lie is metabolic disease, which is a broad term that encompasses everything from insulin resistance through fatty liver disease up until type 2 diabetes.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: I worry the least about metabolic disease and cardiovascular and cerebrovascular disease because one, we have a pretty good handle on the drivers of those diseases. In fact, we have an exceptional handle on the driver of those diseases. And that's half of the equation. The other half of the equation is in response to that, we also have incredible tools for how to combat them. When it comes to reducing the risk of diabetes and cardiovascular disease, or even treating them when present, we know what to do. We've got the tools.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: In the case of cancer, you're dealing with a disease that has two very clear and well understood environmental triggers or behavioral risk factors. The first is smoking. And the second is obesity. Although obesity is simply a proxy, in my view, for the constellation of things that accompanies insulin resistance. Really, I think it's the hyperinsulinemia and the inflammation that often, though not always, accompanies obesity that is driving risk there. Just because you've done those two things does not, for a moment, guarantee you're not going to be diagnosed with cancer. At least 50% of cases of cancer arise in individuals for which there is no observable risk factor. As Bert Vogelstein put it many years ago in a then very controversial paper that I believe was in Science, it's simply about bad luck.

paragraphs.speaker: peter
paragraphs.name: Peter Attia
paragraphs.text: On the neurodegenerative side, I kind of divide these into two categories. On the dementia side, we can really stratify a lot based on genes. A person's genes will play a pretty significant role in risk. Obviously, the ApoE4 gene is the most obvious of these.
